input_candidates.truth_value: Deformation twinning is observed at RT despite high SFE (62.64 mJ/m²), attributed to stress concentration from planar dislocation substructures and short-range ordering (SRO) effect that may lower effective SFE.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Table 2 reports reduction of area (RA): 78 ± 0.5 % at room temperature and 80 ± 1 % at liquid nitrogen temperature for the solution-treated steel.

input_candidates.truth_value: At LNT, crack initiation energy remains ~80–100 J similar to RT, while crack propagation energy drops from ~251 J at RT to ~156 J at LNT, leading to lower total impact energy (254 J vs 331 J).
input_candidates.prediction_value: Table 3 gives true stress, geometrically necessary dislocation density (ρ^GND from EBSD), XRD dislocation density (ρ), and cell block/dislocation cell (CBs/DCs) sizes at engineering strains of 3 %, 10 %, 20 %, 28 %, 38 %, and 55 % at both RT and LNT. At RT, true stress increases from 339 MPa (3 %) to 800 MPa (38 %); ρ^GND (RT) from 0.71×10¹⁴ to 8.99×10¹⁴ m⁻²; ρ (XRD, RT) from 0.64×10¹⁴ to 8.03×10¹⁴ m⁻². At LNT, true stress increases from 598 MPa (3 %) to 1463 MPa (55 %); ρ^GND (LNT) from 0.97×10¹⁴ to 9.73×10¹⁴ m⁻²; ρ (XRD, LNT) from 0.72×10¹⁴ to 13.32×10¹⁴ m⁻². CBs or DCs size at 20 %: 448 ± 74 nm (RT) and 429 ± 76 nm (LNT); at 28 %: 282 ± 56 nm (RT) and 242 ± 54 nm (LNT).

input_candidates.truth_value: The presence of ~3% ferrite refines austenite grains and enhances toughness, but γ/α interfaces become crack initiation sites at LNT, somewhat reducing crack propagation energy.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Fig. 2(b) shows true stress-strain and strain hardening rate curves: RT exhibits two-stage hardening, while LNT exhibits four-stage hardening (stages A, B, C, D). Stage B at LNT is associated with twin activation at annealed twin boundaries; Stage D involves extensive primary and secondary twin activation sustaining high strain hardening.

input_candidates.truth_value: The work hardening at LNT exhibits four stages: A (dislocation accumulation/recovery), B (nearly constant hardening due to twin nucleation at annealed twin boundaries), C (decreasing hardening from dislocation substructure refinement), D (sustained hardening from extensive primary and secondary twinning).
input_candidates.prediction_value: Fig. 4 data: Average {111}<110>γ Schmid factor at RT peaks near 3 % strain (~0.438 initial, ~0.424 at 3 %), while {111}<112>γ Schmid factor at RT decreases then surpasses slip Schmid factor. At LNT, both {111}<110>γ and {111}<112>γ Schmid factors peak at approximately 10 % engineering strain.

input_candidates.truth_value: The density of the steel is 7.18 g/cm³, contributing to lightweight advantage over conventional cryogenic steels.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Fig. 5 and Fig. 7–8 descriptions indicate strong orientation dependence of deformation twinning: type I grains (<001>//TA, deviation ~15°) form dislocation cells and show few twins; type II grains (between <001> and <111>) form single-slip traces and single-orientation twins; type III grains (<111>//TA, deviation ~15°) form double glide traces and multiple-orientation twins. Twins at RT initiate at ~28 % strain along cell block walls; at LNT twins initiate at ~10 % strain at annealed twin boundaries with twin bundle width ~5 nm.

input_candidates.truth_value: Fig.10 compares Charpy impact energy and specific yield strength, showing this steel has higher impact energy than high-carbon lightweight steel and TWIP steel, and superior specific yield strength to 304 stainless steel and low-carbon high-Mn steel.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Fig. 6 indicates that at 28 % strain, type II grains exhibit single slip traces and type III grains exhibit double glide traces; grain subdivision is induced by dislocation glide.
